did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models (specifically comparing Xception, DenseNet169, ResNet152V2, InceptionV3, MobileNetV2, EfficientNetV2 Small, and NASNetMobile) to classify skin lesions (benign Nevus, precancerous Actinic Keratosis, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma). DenseNet169 was selected as the best architecture with 85% accuracy and integrated into a web application for automated classification and analysis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the comparison of seven deep learning models (Xception, DenseNet169, ResNet152V2, InceptionV3, MobileNetV2, EfficientNetV2 Small, and NASNetMobile) for skin lesion classification, resulting in the selection of DenseNet169 (85% accuracy) and its integration into a novel web application. It includes specific methods, results, error analysis, and a developed tool, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Deep Learning-Based Web Application for Automated Skin Lesion Classification and Analysis
brief_summary: This study developed a deep learning-based web application for the automated classification of benign Nevus, precancerous Actinic Keratosis, and malignant Squamous Cell Carcinoma skin lesions. The researchers compared seven deep learning architectures, including Xception, DenseNet169, and ResNet152V2, using a balanced subset of 1,884 images from the ISIC 2019 dataset. DenseNet169 emerged as the best-performing model, achieving an overall classification accuracy of 85%. The selected model was integrated into a Gradio-based web application that provides diagnostic predictions, confidence scores, and feature map visualizations for interpretability. Additionally, the platform incorporates web scraping and summarization techniques to deliver evidence-based dermatological information from trusted sources like the Cleveland Clinic. The resulting tool serves as both a diagnostic support system for clinicians and an educational resource for the general public.

lesion_types_studied.details: Benign lesion class; 628 images in the balanced subset of the ISIC 2019 dataset used for experiments; demonstrated perfect recall (1.00) in the best-performing model.

Precancerous condition class; 628 images in the balanced subset of the ISIC 2019 dataset used for experiments; frequently misclassified as SCC (~18% frequency) and SCC misclassified as AK (~15% frequency).
Malignant condition class; 628 images in the balanced subset of the ISIC 2019 dataset used for experiments; noted for overlapping dermoscopic characteristics with AK.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions: benign Nevus (NV), precancerous Actinic Keratosis (AK), and malignant Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on 'automated skin lesion classification' using deep learning models (Xception, DenseNet169, ResNet152V2, InceptionV3, MobileNetV2, EfficientNetV2 Small, and NASNetMobile) to predict one of three discrete classes. The primary task is multiclass classification, with DenseNet169 achieving 85% accuracy. The web application outputs class predictions and confidence scores for these discrete categories.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset derived from the 2017 and 2018 ISIC challenges, consisting of standardized dermoscopic images. The study used a specific balanced subset of this dataset, excluding images with markings and black outer margins from confocal microscopes to enhance reliability.

use.training: Used for training deep learning models (Xception, DenseNet169, ResNet152V2, InceptionV3, MobileNetV2, EfficientNetV2 Small, and NASNetMobile) with dynamic on-the-fly data augmentation (approx. 10x augmentation yielding ~16,950 samples).
use.validation: Used for validation with modest augmentation (approx. 960 augmented samples generated at train time) to evaluate model performance during training.
use.testing: Used for final testing of the models in its original form (except for pixel rescaling) to ensure rigorous performance evaluation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, and comparisons were made based on these absolute values rather than statistical significance.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained weights from the ImageNet dataset; last 10 layers of feature extraction modules unfrozen and set to trainable, remaining layers frozen.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (images were resized to match model training dimensions during preprocessing).

architecture_details.initial_layers: Conv2D layer (kernel 7x7, stride 2, 64 filters) followed by a 3x3 max pooling layer (stride 2).
architecture_details.blocks: Four dense blocks and three transition layers. Dense blocks consist of convolutional layers with dense connections. Transition layers use batch normalization, a 1x1 convolution, and a 2x2 average pooling.
regularization_techniques: L2 regularization (lambda = 0.01) on the first dense layer.
Dropout (rate = 0.2) after the second dense layer.
Gaussian noise layer (standard deviation = 0.001) introduced before the final layer.

data_augmentation.training: Random rotations (up to 30 degrees), horizontal/vertical shifts (up to 50%), shearing (factor 0.5), zooming (factor 0.5), horizontal flipping, nearest neighbor fill.
data_augmentation.validation: Random rotations (up to 30 degrees), shifts (up to 30%), shearing (factor 0.3), zooming (factor 0.3), horizontal flipping.

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to a standard size of 256 × 256 pixels
Pixel value normalization within the range [0, 1]

Dynamic on-the-fly data augmentation for training set: random rotations (up to 30 degrees), horizontal/vertical shifts (up to 50%), shearing (factor 0.5), zooming (factor 0.5), and horizontal flipping

Dynamic on-the-fly data augmentation for validation set: random rotations (up to 30 degrees), horizontal/vertical shifts (up to 30%), shearing (factor 0.3), zooming (factor 0.3), and horizontal flipping
Nearest-neighbor filling to handle blank spaces generated by transformations
Pixel rescaling for the test set

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied up to 30 degrees for both training and validation sets.
Applied up to 50% of image size for training; restricted to 30% for validation.
Applied with a factor of 0.5 for training; restricted to a factor of 0.3 for validation.
Applied with a factor of 0.5 for training; restricted to a factor of 0.3 for validation.
Applied to both training and validation sets.
Used to fill blank spaces generated by geometric transformations to maintain image integrity.

Augmentations were applied dynamically during training without saving individual augmented image files. Training data was augmented approximately 10 times (yielding ~16,950 samples from 1,695 original images). Validation data underwent more modest augmentation (yielding ~960 samples from 96 original images).

conclusions: DenseNet169 was identified as the best-performing deep learning architecture for skin lesion classification, achieving an overall accuracy of 85% on a balanced subset of the ISIC 2019 dataset.
The developed web application serves as both a diagnostic support system for dermatologists and an educational resource for the general public.
The integration of interpretability techniques, such as feature map visualization, enhances diagnostic clarity and allows users to investigate how the model processes images.

The application facilitates early intervention and increases awareness regarding skin health by combining automated classification with automatic extraction of evidence-based medical information from trusted sources like the Cleveland Clinic.

While DenseNet169 demonstrated high efficacy, particularly with perfect recall for benign Nevus (NV) lesions, challenges remain in distinguishing between precancerous Actinic Keratosis (AK) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) due to overlapping dermoscopic characteristics.
Other tested models, such as EfficientNetV2 Small and NASNetMobile, performed poorly or marginally, indicating that architecture selection is critical for dermoscopic image classification tasks.
important_remarks: The study relies on a balanced subset of the ISIC 2019 dataset, which consists mainly of dermoscopic images from lighter populations, introducing significant racial/skin tone bias and limiting applicability to dark-skinned individuals.
The dataset size is small (628 images per class), which may limit the model's ability to generalize, particularly for complex and heterogeneous lesions like AK and SCC.

There is a high rate of confusion between precancerous Actinic Keratosis (AK) and malignant Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC), with ~18% of AK misclassified as SCC and ~15% of SCC misclassified as AK, due to overlapping dermoscopic characteristics.
The model achieved perfect recall (1.00) for benign Nevus (NV) but lower performance for AK and SCC, indicating difficulty in distinguishing early-stage or borderline lesions.

The dataset included images with markings and black outer margins from confocal microscopes, which were removed to enhance reliability, potentially affecting the representativeness of the training data.
No formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or statistical significance tests were reported; model comparisons were based on absolute metric values.
External validation has not yet been performed; the authors plan future collaborations with academic hospitals for retrospective benchmarking and prospective deployment to assess real-world utility.
The web application incorporates web scraping from the Cleveland Clinic for medical information, which may introduce risks if the external sources change or become unavailable.
The study did not report performance metrics stratified by Fitzpatrick skin types, despite acknowledging the need for fairness across diverse populations.
EfficientNetV2 Small and NASNetMobile performed poorly (accuracy 0.43 and 0.67 respectively), highlighting the critical importance of architecture selection for this specific task.
